questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Some parts of a few sentences have been jumbled up, and labelled A, B, C and D. Select the option that gives the correct sequence in which these parts can be rearranged to form a meaningful and grammatically correct sentence. The Ramayana is one of the largest ancient A. 24,000 verses divided into seven Khanda (parts) B. the first and the seventh being later additions. C. epics in world literature. It consists of nearly D. It belongs to the genre of Itihasa, narratives of past events, interspersed with teachings on the goals of human life.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is a: CABD When rearranged, these parts form a coherent and meaningful sentence: "The Ramayana is one of the largest ancient epics in world literature. It consists of nearly 24,000 verses divided into seven Khanda (parts), the first and the seventh being later additions. It belongs to the genre of Itihasa, narratives of past events, interspersed with teachings on the goals of human life." Therefore, the correct sequence is CABD.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Improve the underlined part of the sentence. Choose ‘No improvement’ as an answer if the sentence is grammatically correct. The actors i̲s̲ ̲t̲o̲ ̲s̲h̲o̲o̲t̲ the scene three times.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct form of the verb should be "had to shoot" to indicate a past obligation or requirement. had to shoot: This option uses the past tense form "had to," indicating a past obligation or requirement. It correctly conveys that the actors were required to shoot the scene three times in the past. Therefore, the corrected sentence would be: “The actors had to shoot the scene three times.”

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the word that is closest in meaning (SYNONYM) to the word given below. Frequent
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} img {max-width:100% !important} *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The word that is closest in meaning (synonym) to "Frequent" is: a) Intermittent • Frequent: Happening or occurring often; regular or habitual in occurrence. • Intermittent means occurring at irregular intervals but repeatedly, which is the closest match to frequent, meaning happening often. • Ceasing: Stopping or coming to an end; halting. • Broken: Separated into parts or pieces; not whole or complete; disrupted or interrupted. • Inconsistent: Not staying the same throughout; lacking in uniformity or regularity; contradictory or conflicting.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following question, out of the given four alternatives, select the alternative which best expresses the meaning of the Idiom/Phrase. To jump out of one’s skin
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The idiom "To jump out of one’s skin" means to be extremely surprised or startled. Therefore, the alternative that best expresses the meaning of this idiom is: d) To be extremely surprised

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate meaning of the given idiom. Good grief
options: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} An expression of surprise or frustration
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The idiom "Good grief" is an expression of surprise or frustration, often used to convey astonishment or exasperation. So, the most appropriate meaning of the given idiom is: d) An expression of surprise or frustration

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the word that can substitute the given group of words. One who goes on foot
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The word that can substitute the given group of words "One who goes on foot" is: c) Pedestrian • Cynic: A person who believes that people are motivated purely by self-interest rather than acting for honorable or unselfish reasons. • Hypochondriac: A person who is excessively anxious about their health and often believes they are suffering from serious medical conditions, even when there is no evidence of illness. • Pedestrian: A person who travels on foot. • Vigilant: Watchful, attentive, or alert, especially to potential danger or threats.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Some parts of a few sentences have been jumbled up, and labelled A, B, C and D. Select the option that gives the correct sequence in which these parts can be rearranged to form a meaningful and grammatically correct sentence. Mr. Summers spoke frequently A. the one that had been constructed when the first people settled down to make a village here B. liked to upset even as much tradition as was represented by the black box. There was a C. to the villagers about making a new box, but no one D. story that the present box had been made with some pieces of the box that had preceded it
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is c: CBDA The correct sequence in which these parts can be rearranged to form a meaningful and grammatically correct sentence is: C. to the villagers about making a new box, but no one B. liked to upset even as much tradition as was represented by the black box. There was a D. story that the present box had been made with some pieces of the box that had preceded it A. the one that had been constructed when the first people settled down to make a village here So, the correct option is: CBDA

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the incorrectly spelt word.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The incorrectly spelled word is: d) CONSCENT The correct spelling is "CONSENT." Splendid: Impressive; magnificent; excellent. Meagre: Lacking in quantity or quality; inadequate; scanty. Marriageable: Suitable or eligible for marriage; of an age or condition suitable for marriage. Consent: Permission or agreement, especially when given by someone in authority.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the correctly spelt word.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correctly spelled word is: a) MINARET Minaret: A tall, slender tower, typically part of a mosque, with a balcony from which a muezzin calls Muslims to prayer. "PERTINENT": Relevant or applicable to a particular matter; appropriate. Occurred: Happened; took place; transpired. "RELEVANT": Closely connected or appropriate to what is being done or considered; pertinent or applicable.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate antonym of the given word. TARDY
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is c: PROMPT "TARDY" means late or delayed. PROMPT: Done without delay; on time; punctual. OVERDUE: Not having been paid, returned, or dealt with when expected or required; past the due date. BELATED: Coming or happening later than should have been the case; delayed or tardy. DELINQUENT: Failing in or neglectful of a duty or obligation; showing a tendency to commit crime, particularly minor crime.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the correct passive form of the given sentence. Ravish likes chocolate.
options: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Chocolate were liked by Ravish.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Chocolate is liked by Ravish.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Chocolate is being liked by Ravish.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is c: “Chocolate is liked by Ravish.” Active voice ---- Passive voice Subject + V(s/es) + Object ---- Object + is/am/are + V3 + by + Subject Therefore, the correct passive form of the given sentence is: “Chocolate is liked by Ravish.”

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate 'one word' for the expression given below. Place where bees are kept.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate one-word expression for the place where bees are kept is: d) Apiary Net: A fabric made of threads or cords woven or knotted together at regular intervals, used typically for catching fish or other animals. Hive: A structure for housing a colony of honeybees, typically made of wooden boxes or frames with parallel rows of honeycombs. Aviary: A large enclosure or cage, usually made of wire or mesh, for keeping birds. Apiary: A place where beehives are kept, often specifically designed for the raising and production of bees and honey.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Fill in the blank with an appropriate option. I dump the tray on the side and start ______ up the dishwasher.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank is: loading • Loading: Placing items, such as dishes or clothes, into a machine or container in preparation for operation or transportation. • Tantalizing: Tempting or teasing by arousing expectations or desires, often without intending to satisfy them fully. • Alienating: Causing someone to feel isolated or estranged; making someone unfriendly or indifferent. • Upbringing: The care and training a child receives while growing up; the way in which someone is raised or brought up.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Parts of the following sentence are given as options. Identify the part that contains a grammatical error. If there is no error, select 'No error'. Ashwatthama, Drona’s / only son, has had developed / a fondness for lavish living.
options: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} a fondness for lavish living.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} only son, has had developed
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The part of the sentence that contains a grammatical error is: b) only son, has had developed "Has had" is a compound verb form that typically indicates an action that started in the past and continued up to the present. For example, "He has had breakfast" suggests that he ate breakfast at some point in the past, and the effects of eating breakfast are relevant in the present. Therefore, the corrected sentence would be: "Ashwatthama, Drona’s only son, has developed a fondness for lavish living."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate one-word substitution for the given group of words. An argument or statement seeming reasonable or probable
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate one-word substitution for the given group of words "An argument or statement seeming reasonable or probable" is: c) Plausible • Plausible: Seeming reasonable or probable; appearing to be true or credible; believable. • Corrigible: Capable of being corrected, reformed, or improved; amendable. • Congruent: Agreeing or corresponding; harmonious or consistent; having the same size and shape. • Incorrigible: Not capable of being corrected, reformed, or improved; resistant to change or reform; stubbornly continuing bad behavior.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Select the most appropriate antonym of the given word. Sporadic
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate antonym of the given word "Sporadic" is: b) Systematic "Sporadic" describes something happening irregularly or occasionally. "Scattered" means spread or distributed irregularly or loosely. "Systematic" refers to something characterized by orderliness or methodical arrangement. "Random" means happening without method or conscious decision, occurring without a specific pattern. "Rare" describes something that does not occur very often.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the option that is the correct indirect form of the sentence. “Who is it?” Vikram asked, his euphoria fading.
options: As his euphoria faded, Vikram asks as to who was it.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} With his euphoria fading, Vikram asked who it was.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} With a faded euphoria Vikram asked who is it.
*{overflow-wrap: break-word;} With his euphoria faded, Vikram asked who it is.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct indirect form of the given sentence is: b) With his euphoria fading, Vikram asked who it was. Direct speech ---- Indirect speech Simple present tense ---- Simple past tense Is/am/are ---- was/were Therefore, the correct indirect form of the sentence is: “With his euphoria fading, Vikran asked who is was.”

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Choose the word that is opposite in meaning to the given word. Temerity
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The word that is opposite in meaning to "Temerity" is: a) Diffidence "Temerity" means excessive confidence or boldness; audacity. "Diffidence" means shyness or modesty; a lack of confidence or self-assurance. Audacity: Boldness or daring, especially with confident or arrogant disregard for personal safety, conventional thought, or other restrictions. Poison: A substance that is capable of causing illness or death; typically when ingested, inhaled, or absorbed into the body in sufficient quantities. Assurance: Confidence or certainty in one's own abilities; a promise or guarantee given with confidence; a formal declaration intended to give confidence; a pledge.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Fill in the blank with an appropriate option. Lecanemab was recently reported to have a small but ______ effect in reducing cognitive decline.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is b: significant "Significant" means important or notable; having a considerable or meaningful impact. This implies that even though the effect may be small in magnitude, it is considered significant or important in the context of reducing cognitive decline. Therefore, "significant" is an appropriate word choice to convey the impact of Lecanemab in the sentence. Microscopic: Extremely small; invisible to the naked eye; involving or concerned with minute details or precision. Optional: Left to one's choice; not mandatory; available as a choice but not required. Marginal: Small or minimal in amount, effect, or significance; on the edge or periphery; not central or important.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} Find the part of the given sentence that has an error in it. If there is no error, choose ‘No error’. Democrats are often more receptive and accommodating of foreign people and cultures.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The correct answer is b: and accommodating of The phrase "and accommodating of" is indeed incorrect. The correct phrase should be "and accommodative of." Correct sentence: "Democrats are often more receptive and accommodative of foreign people and cultures."

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. With his slight build and his simple white clothes, Narad (1) ________ harmless, but he had quite a (2) ________. He had powerful family connections and was a (3) ________ devotee of Lord Vishnu. His favourite (4) ________ was to travel from court to court and world to world, collecting gossip and (5) ________ mayhem. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 1.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 1 is: d) looked Saw: The past tense of "see," meaning to perceive with the eyes; to discern or understand. Observed: To notice or perceive something; to watch carefully or attentively. Watched: To look at or observe attentively over a period of time; to keep under close or continuous observation. Looked: To direct one's gaze in a particular direction; to turn one's eyes toward something or someone.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. With his slight build and his simple white clothes, Narad (1) ________ harmless, but he had quite a (2) ________. He had powerful family connections and was a (3) ________ devotee of Lord Vishnu. His favourite (4) ________ was to travel from court to court and world to world, collecting gossip and (5) ________ mayhem. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 2.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 2 is: b) reputation Reputation: The beliefs or opinions that are generally held about someone or something. Disapproval: The act of disagreeing with or condemning something; the absence of approval or favorable opinion. Dismay: A sudden loss of courage or resolution from fear or anxiety; a sudden and complete loss of confidence. Censure: The expression of formal disapproval; harsh criticism or condemnation.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. With his slight build and his simple white clothes, Narad (1) ________ harmless, but he had quite a (2) ________. He had powerful family connections and was a (3) ________ devotee of Lord Vishnu. His favourite (4) ________ was to travel from court to court and world to world, collecting gossip and (5) ________ mayhem. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 3.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 3 is: a) formidable The word "Formidable" means inspiring fear or respect through being impressively large, powerful, intense, or capable. Pleasant: Giving a sense of happy satisfaction or enjoyment; pleasing; agreeable. Brooding: Showing deep unhappiness of thought; appearing dark or threatening; appearing deep in thought; preoccupied with depressing, morbid, or painful memories or thoughts. Peasant: A poor farmer of low social status who owns or rents a small piece of land for cultivation (historically).

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. With his slight build and his simple white clothes, Narad (1) ________ harmless, but he had quite a (2) ________. He had powerful family connections and was a (3) ________ devotee of Lord Vishnu. His favourite (4) ________ was to travel from court to court and world to world, collecting gossip and (5) ________ mayhem. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 4.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 4 is: Activity Activity: A situation in which a lot of things are happening or being done. Movement: A group of people working together to advance their shared political, social, or artistic ideas or aims. Action: The fact or process of doing something, typically to achieve an aim. Bustle: A flurry of activity; energetic and noisy movement or activity.

questions.question: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} In the following passage some words have been deleted. Fill in the blanks with the help of the alternatives given. Select the most appropriate option for each number. With his slight build and his simple white clothes, Narad (1) ________ harmless, but he had quite a (2) ________. He had powerful family connections and was a (3) ________ devotee of Lord Vishnu. His favourite (4) ________ was to travel from court to court and world to world, collecting gossip and (5) ________ mayhem. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 5.
questions.explanation: *{overflow-wrap: break-word;} The most appropriate option to fill in the blank No. 5 is: b) spreading • Spreading: Causing something to extend over a larger area or among a larger number of people. • Scattering: Throwing in various random directions; dispersing. • Diffusing: Spreading over a wide area or among a large number of people. • Advancing: Moving forward in a purposeful way; promoting or helping to progress.
